name: Global Preferences
description: Manages user-level preferences persisted to ~/.code-workbench/settings.json (outside VS Code settings). Covers claude command path, yolo args, default model/effort/yolo, custom system prompt injections, response/comment language, and open-on-startup. Provides sync and async loaders plus a file watcher for live reload.
guidelines: Prefer globalPrefs values over VS Code workspace config for claude command and yolo args — global prefs win.
Use watchGlobalPrefs() to keep SessionManager.globalPrefs in sync; never cache a stale copy across sessions.

GlobalPrefs.language ('auto' default) and .commentLanguage ('inherit' default) are free strings, not a closed enum — sanitize with language.ts's sanitizeLanguage() (trim, single line, ~64 char cap) since they land in a system prompt, not a CLI flag.

languagePromptBody() in language.ts turns the two prefs into a prompt clause; SessionManager.buildLaunch() prepends it to extraPrompts (sessions.ts, extraPrompts assembly) so it renders before user-defined prompt snippets, which can still override it by coming later.
Changing language/commentLanguage only affects sessions launched after the change — it is not pushed to already-running sessions.
anti_patterns: Do not store workbench-level user preferences in VS Code settings — they would silently differ per workspace. Use the shared ~/.code-workbench/settings.json.
Do not treat language/commentLanguage as a closed enum when validating — a custom user string (e.g. a dialect not in the LANGUAGES catalog) must still normalize and inject correctly.
decisions: Stored outside VS Code to be accessible to the Electron app and other tooling that reads the same config directory.

Language preference is two independent fields (reply language vs. comment language) rather than one, because users often want prose in their native language but code comments in English; commentLanguage defaults to 'inherit' (same as language).
